title: 🤰 PREECLAMPSIA VS GESTATIONAL HTN
content:
Hypertension after 20 weeks is a major Canadian exam topic.

FeatureGestational HTNPreeclampsia
BP> 140/90> 140/90
ProteinuriaNonePositive (or organ failure)
EdemaCommonSignificant

⚠️ Exam Trap: In Canada, the definition of Preeclampsia no longer strictly requires proteinuria if there is End-organ dysfunction (low platelets, high Cr, or visual symptoms). If BP is > 160/110, it is Severe!

quiz.q: What is the only definitive treatment for severe preeclampsia?
quiz.a: Delivery of the fetus and placenta.

title: 🩸 POSTPARTUM HEMORRHAGE (PPH)
content:
The 4 T's mnemonic is the gold standard for MCCQE1.

1. Tone: Uterine Atony (Most common - 80%).
2. Tissue: Retained Products.
3. Trauma: Lacerations.
4. Thrombin: Coagulopathy.

⚠️ Exam Trap: The first step for a boggy uterus is Uterine Massage and Oxytocin. If the uterus is firm but bleeding continues, the answer is Trauma/Laceration.

quiz.q: What is the most common cause of PPH?

title: ⚖️ MAID (MEDICAL ASSISTANCE IN DYING)
content:
Specific to Canadian law. Two clinicians must confirm eligibility.

• Criteria: 18+ years, capable of decision, grievous and irremediable medical condition.
• Process: Must be informed of palliative care options first.

⚠️ Exam Trap: You cannot suggest MAID to a patient out of the blue. The patient must initiate the request. Also, clinicians have the right to conscientious objection but must provide a 'formal referral' in many provinces.

quiz.q: Can a person request MAID through an advance directive in Canada currently?
quiz.a: No, the patient must have capacity at the time of the request (current law).

title: ⚖️ INFORMED CONSENT
content:
Consent must be voluntary, capable, specific, and informed.

• Disclosure: You must disclose what a 'reasonable person' in the patient's position would want to know.
• Emergency: If no SDM is available and it's life-saving, you treat without consent.

⚠️ Exam Trap: A signed consent form is evidence of consent, but not consent itself. If the patient changes their mind verbally after signing, the verbal refusal trumps the form.

quiz.q: What is the age of majority for medical consent in Ontario?
quiz.a: There is no fixed age (Mature Minor doctrine applies).

title: 🧠 DEPRESSION VS ADJUSTMENT DISORDER
content:
Distinguishing the two depends on the time and severity.

FeatureAdjustment DisorderMajor Depression (MDD)
TriggerIdentifiable stressorMay or may not exist
TimingWithin 3 months of stress> 2 weeks
CriteriaSub-threshold for MDDFull SIGECAPS (5/9)

⚠️ Exam Trap: Grief (bereavement) is normal. It becomes MDD only if it's persistent, causes severe functional impairment, or involves suicidality/worthlessness.

quiz.q: What is the first-line treatment for Adjustment Disorder?

title: 🧠 SCHIZOPHRENIA TIME-LINE
content:
Psychosis is a spectrum based on duration.

• < 1 Month: Brief Psychotic Disorder.
• 1 - 6 Months: Schizophreniform.
• > 6 Months: Schizophrenia.


⚠️ Exam Trap: If mood symptoms (Depression/Mania) are present, it's Schizoaffective only if psychosis persists for 2 weeks WITHOUT mood symptoms.

quiz.q: What is the main side effect of Clozapine that requires mandatory blood monitoring?

title: 👶 DEVELOPMENTAL RED FLAGS
content:
Must-know milestones for the MCCQE1.

• 6 Months: Not sitting with support.
• 9 Months: No pincer grasp.
• 12 Months: No babbling or pointing.
• 18 Months: Not walking or < 15 words.

⚠️ Exam Trap: Loss of previously acquired skills (Regression) is the biggest red flag and requires urgent workup for metabolic or neurodegenerative disease.

quiz.q: At what age should a child normally sit without support?

title: 🧠 PEDIATRIC MENINGITIS
content:
Pathogens change by age. Treatment must be rapid.

AgePathogenTx
NewbornGBS, E. coli, ListeriaAmp + Gent/Cefotaxime
> 1 MonthS. pneumo, N. meningitidisCeftriaxone + Vanco

⚠️ Exam Trap: Kernig and Brudzinski signs are often absent in infants. Bulging fontanelle and irritability are more common clues in neonates.

quiz.q: What is the most common cause of neonatal meningitis?

title: 🧬 CANCER SCREENING (CANADA)
content:
High-yield summary of Task Force guidelines.

• Cervical: Ages 25-65 (Cytology every 3 years).
• Breast: Ages 50-74 (Mammogram every 2 years).
• Colon: Ages 50-74 (FIT/FOBT every 2 years or Colonoscopy every 10).

⚠️ Exam Trap: In Canada, we generally do NOT screen for Prostate cancer with PSA routinely unless the patient requests it after a 'Shared Decision Making' discussion.

quiz.q: When do you stop screening for cervical cancer in Canada?
quiz.a: At age 65 if the last 3 results were negative.

title: 🧓 FALLS IN ELDERLY (IS HAME)
content:
A systematic approach to a falling patient.

• I: Infection (UTI/Pneumonia).
• S: Situational (Loose rugs).
• H: Hematologic (Anemia).
• A: Activity/Arrythmia.
• M: Meds (Benzos/Antihypertensives).
• E: Electrolytes.

⚠️ Exam Trap: The best predictor of a future fall is a history of a previous fall. Always check 'Get up and Go' test.

quiz.q: What is a common medication class that increases fall risk in the elderly?

title: 🧠 GIANT CELL ARTERITIS (GCA)
content:
Emergency! Risk of Permanent Blindness.

• Symptoms: New headache, Scalp tenderness, Jaw Claudication (pain with chewing).
• Lab: ESR > 50 mm/hr (High sensitivity).

⚠️ Exam Trap: If suspicion is high, start High-dose Steroids IMMEDIATELY. Do not wait for the Temporal Artery Biopsy results!

quiz.q: What is the most common serious complication of GCA?

title: 🧪 NEPHROTIC VS NEPHRITIC
content:
FeatureNephroticNephritic
Proteinuria> 3.5 g/dayVariable
HematuriaAbsentRBC Casts
EdemaSevereMild

⚠️ Exam Trap: Minimal Change Disease is the #1 cause in kids (Tx: Steroids). In adults, think Membranous or DM. RBC Casts = Nephritis!

quiz.q: What is the hallmark finding of a nephritic syndrome on urinalysis?

title: 🧩 INTUSSUSCEPTION
content:
Telescoping of bowel, usually in children 6-36 months.

• Triad: Colicky pain, Sausage-shaped mass, Currant Jelly Stool.


⚠️ Exam Trap: The diagnosis and initial treatment are the same: Air or Contrast Enema (Radiographic reduction).

quiz.q: What is the classic stool appearance in intussusception?

title: 🦠 PELVIC INFLAMMATORY DISEASE (PID)
content:
Infection of the upper genital tract.
• Signs: Lower abdominal pain + Cervical Motion Tenderness (Chandelier sign).
• Complication: Fitz-Hugh-Curtis (Liver capsule inflammation).

⚠️ Exam Trap: Treatment must be Ceftriaxone + Doxycycline + Metronidazole. You must also treat the partner to prevent re-infection!

quiz.q: What is a long-term complication of untreated PID?
quiz.a: Infertility or Ectopic Pregnancy (due to tubal scarring).

title: ❄️ HYPOTHERMIA ECG
content:
When core temp is < 35°C.
• Look for: Osborn Waves (J-waves).
• They are positive deflections at the end of the QRS complex.


⚠️ Exam Trap: A patient is not dead until they are Warm and Dead. Continue resuscitation until re-warmed to 32-35°C.

quiz.q: At what core temperature is a person defined as hypothermic?

title: 🧠 SIGECAPS (DEPRESSION)
content:
Must have 5 out of 9 for ≥ 2 weeks (including low mood or anhedonia).

S: Sleep (High/Low)
I: Interest (Loss)
G: Guilt
E: Energy (Low)
C: Concentration
A: Appetite (High/Low)
P: Psychomotor agitation/retardation
S: Suicidal ideation

⚠️ Exam Trap: Always screen for MANIA before starting SSRIs to avoid triggering a bipolar switch.

quiz.q: How long must symptoms be present to diagnose MDD?

title: 🦴 SEPTIC ARTHRITIS
content:
Medical emergency! Usually S. aureus.
• Presentation: Hot, swollen, exquisitely tender joint + Fever.
• Joint Fluid: WBC > 50,000 (Predominantly Neutrophils).

⚠️ Exam Trap: The first step is Arthrocentesis (Aspiration) before starting antibiotics! Antibiotics in the joint will make the culture negative.

quiz.q: What is the most common cause of septic arthritis in young sexually active adults?

title: 🦋 THYROID STORM
content:
Life-threatening thyrotoxicosis.
• Signs: Fever > 40°C, Tachycardia, Agitation, Heart Failure.
• Tx: 4 P's: Propranolol, PTU, Prednisone, Potassium Iodide.

⚠️ Exam Trap: Give PTU (or Methimazole) before giving Iodine. If you give Iodine first, it will act as fuel for more hormone synthesis!

quiz.q: Why is Propranolol used in thyroid storm?
quiz.a: To control heart rate and block peripheral conversion of T4 to T3.

title: 🤰 MAGNESIUM SULFATE TOXICITY
content:
Used for neuroprotection and eclampsia prophylaxis.

SignMg Level
Loss of DTRsFirst sign
Resp. DepressionLate/Severe
Cardiac ArrestVery High

⚠️ Exam Trap: The antidote for Mg toxicity is Calcium Gluconate. Always check reflexes and urine output while on Mg.

quiz.q: What is the first physical sign of Magnesium toxicity?

title: 🧂 SIADH VS DI
content:
FeatureSIADHDiabetes Insipidus
Urine OsmHigh (>100)Low (<100)
Serum SodiumLow (Hyponatremia)High (Hypernatremia)
TxFluid RestrictDesmopressin (Central)

⚠️ Exam Trap: Small Cell Lung Cancer is a classic trigger for SIADH. Lithium is a classic trigger for Nephrogenic DI!

quiz.q: What is the first-line treatment for SIADH?

title: 🟡 PANCREATIC CANCER
content:
Key Sign: Painless Jaundice + Weight loss.

• Courvoisier Sign: Palpable, non-tender, enlarged gallbladder.
• Trousseau Sign: Migratory thrombophlebitis.
• Dx: CT Scan (Initial), CA 19-9 (Marker for monitoring).

⚠️ Exam Trap: In MCCQE1, if you see 'Painless Jaundice' in an elderly smoker, the answer is Pancreatic Cancer until proven otherwise. Do not wait for labs!

quiz.q: What is the most common location for pancreatic cancer?

title: ⚡ CHADS-65 (ATRIAL FIBRILLATION)
content:
Canadian Specific Algorithm for anticoagulation in AFib.

CriteriaPoints
C: Congestive Heart Failure1
H: Hypertension1
A: Age ≥ 65Mandatory OAC
D: Diabetes1
S: Stroke/TIA history1

⚠️ Exam Trap: In Canada, if the patient is ≥ 65 years old, they automatically need an Oral Anticoagulant (OAC) regardless of other scores! DOACs (Apixaban, etc.) are preferred over Warfarin.

quiz.q: When is Warfarin preferred over DOACs in AFib?
quiz.a: Valvular AFib (Mechanical heart valves or Moderate-Severe Mitral Stenosis).

title: 🤰 METHOTREXATE FOR ECTOPIC
content:
Medical management criteria for stable ectopic pregnancy.

Absolute Contraindications:
• Hemodynamic instability (Rupture).
• IUP (Intrauterine pregnancy) present.
• Breastfeeding.

RequirementCut-off
Fetal Heart ToneAbsent
Mass Size< 3.5 - 4 cm
β-hCG Level< 5000 IU/L

⚠️ Exam Trap: If β-hCG is > 5000 or mass is large, MTX failure rate is high. The correct answer in these cases is Laparoscopic Salpingostomy/Salpingectomy.

quiz.q: What must be avoided for 72 hours after Methotrexate administration?
quiz.a: Alcohol and Folic Acid (to prevent interference with the drug).

title: 🦋 THYROID UPTAKE PATTERNS
content:
Differentiating causes of Hyperthyroidism using Radioiodine (RAIU).

PatternDiagnosis
Diffuse High UptakeGrave's Disease
Patchy/Nodal HighToxic Multinodular Goiter
Low/Zero UptakeThyroiditis or Factitious

⚠️ Exam Trap: De Quervain’s (Subacute) Thyroiditis presents with a Tender/Painful thyroid and Low uptake. Grave's is never painful!

quiz.q: What is the most likely diagnosis for a patient with hyperthyroid labs, low uptake, and a painful neck?

title: 👁️ ACUTE ANGLE CLOSURE GLAUCOMA
content:
Vision-threatening emergency.
• Symptoms: Sudden eye pain, Halos around lights, Nausea/Vomiting.
• Exam: Mid-dilated fixed pupil, 'Steamy' cornea, Rock-hard eye.


⚠️ Exam Trap: Avoid Atropine or any dilating drops! They will worsen the angle closure. Treatment includes Timolol, Pilocarpine, and Acetazolamide.

quiz.q: What is the definitive treatment for acute angle closure glaucoma?

title: 🦠 SYPHILIS STAGES
content:
Caused by Treponema pallidum.

• Primary: Painless Chancre.
• Secondary: Rash (Palms/Soles), Condyloma lata.
• Tertiary: Gummas, Tabes dorsalis, Argyll Robertson pupil.

⚠️ Exam Trap: For Primary Syphilis, Darkfield microscopy is the fastest diagnosis. VDRL/RPR can be false negative in the first few weeks!

quiz.q: What is the gold standard treatment for all stages of Syphilis?

title: 🩸 MENTZER INDEX
content:
Differentiating Iron Deficiency from Thalassemia in microcytic anemia.

MCV / RBC count

• > 13: Likely Iron Deficiency.
• < 13: Likely Thalassemia.

⚠️ Exam Trap: In Thalassemia, the RBC count is often High (the body overproduces small cells), while in Iron Deficiency, the RBC count is Low.

quiz.q: What is the next best test to confirm Iron Deficiency Anemia?

title: 💓 STILL'S MURMUR (BENIGN)
content:
The most common innocent murmur in children.

• Sound: Musical, vibratory, mid-systolic.
• Location: LLSB (Lower left sternal border).
• Position: Louder when Supine (lying down).

⚠️ Exam Trap: Benign murmurs decrease in intensity when the child stands up. If the murmur gets louder with standing, think HOCM!

quiz.q: At what age is Still's murmur most commonly heard?

title: 👃 EPISTAXIS (NOSEBLEED)
content:
90% are Anterior (Kiesselbach’s Plexus).

Management Steps:
1. Blow nose + Continuous pressure (15 min).
2. Topical vasoconstrictor (Oxymetazoline).
3. Silver Nitrate cautery (if source visible).

⚠️ Exam Trap: If bleeding is bilateral, massive, or goes down the throat, think Posterior Bleed (Sphenopalatine artery). This needs Posterior Packing and mandatory Admission (risk of apnea).

quiz.q: What is the most common site for anterior epistaxis?

title: 🦶 OTTAWA ANKLE RULES
content:
A high-yield tool to avoid unnecessary X-rays.

X-ray is only needed if:
1. Bone tenderness at Posterior Edge of malleolus (last 6cm).
2. Bone tenderness at Navicular or 5th Metatarsal.
3. Inability to weight bear (4 steps).


⚠️ Exam Trap: The Ottawa rules are highly Sensitive (99%). If they are negative, you can safely rule out a fracture without an X-ray.

quiz.q: Do Ottawa Ankle Rules apply to patients under age 5?
quiz.a: No, they are generally validated for patients > 5 years old.

title: ✋ OA VS RA (HAND DIFFERENTIATION)
content:
A classic Canadian exam comparison for joint pain.

FeatureOsteoarthritis (OA)Rheumatoid (RA)
Stiffness< 30 min (Morning)> 60 min
JointsDIP / PIP (Bouchard/Heberden)MCP / PIP (Symmetry)
Ulnar DriftAbsentPresent

SVG Visual: Hand Joints

⚠️ Exam Trap: RA never affects the DIP joints. If the question mentions pain in the DIPs (fingertips), the answer is OA or Psoriatic Arthritis, not RA!

quiz.q: Which hand joint is characteristically spared in Rheumatoid Arthritis?

title: 🩸 ANEMIA LAB INTERPRETATION
content:
Sorting microcytic anemia using Ferritin and TIBC.

LabIron DeficiencyChronic Disease
FerritinLow (< 30)Normal / High
TIBCHighLow
% SaturationVery LowLow

⚠️ Exam Trap: Ferritin is an Acute Phase Reactant. In a patient with inflammation (like RA or infection), a 'Normal' ferritin doesn't rule out iron deficiency. Look for the Soluble Transferrin Receptor if available!

quiz.q: What is the single most specific test for iron deficiency anemia?

title: 🤰 GBS SCREENING (CANADA)
content:
Group B Streptococcus (GBS) management in pregnancy.

• Screening: All women at 35-37 weeks (Vaginal-rectal swab).
• Treatment: Intrapartum IV Penicillin G (or Ampicillin).

⚠️ Exam Trap: If a woman has GBS Bacteriuria (any concentration) during the current pregnancy, she automatically gets IV antibiotics during labor. No swab needed at 35-37 weeks!

quiz.q: What is the first-line antibiotic for GBS prophylaxis during labor?

title: 🌬️ PFT INTERPRETATION (OBSTRUCTIVE)
content:
Using the FEV1/FVC ratio to diagnose lung disease.

• Obstructive (Asthma/COPD): FEV1/FVC < 0.70.
• Restrictive (Fibrosis): FEV1/FVC is Normal or High (Both low, but ratio stays same).

⚠️ Exam Trap: In Asthma, the obstruction is Reversible (>12% increase in FEV1 after bronchodilator). In COPD, the obstruction is NOT fully reversible.

quiz.q: What FEV1/FVC ratio cut-off defines an obstructive lung pattern?

title: 🧠 ANTIPSYCHOTIC METABOLIC MONITORING
content:
Second-generation antipsychotics (SGA) and metabolic syndrome.

• Highest Risk: Clozapine and Olanzapine (Weight gain/Diabetes).
• Lowest Risk: Aripiprazole and Ziprasidone.

⚠️ Exam Trap: If a patient on Olanzapine gains 5kg in a month, the next step is NOT to stop the drug immediately, but to monitor Fasting Glucose and Lipid profile and consider switching.

quiz.q: Which two antipsychotics have the highest risk for weight gain and diabetes?

title: ⚖️ MEDICAL RECORDS OWNERSHIP
content:
A very common ethics question in Canada.

• Who owns the record? The Physician or the Hospital (The physical/digital file).
• Who owns the information? The Patient.

⚠️ Exam Trap: A patient has a legal right to Access and Copy their records, but they cannot take the Original file. You can charge a 'reasonable fee' for copying.

quiz.q: Can a physician withhold medical records because the patient hasn't paid their bill?
quiz.a: No, this is considered professional misconduct.

title: 🧪 NEPHROTIC SYNDROME (KIDS)
content:
Most common cause in children: Minimal Change Disease.

• Classic Presentation: Periorbital edema (worse in morning), Scrotal edema, 4+ Proteinuria.
• Treatment: Oral Corticosteroids (Prednisone).

⚠️ Exam Trap: In kids, we do NOT biopsy first. We give steroids. If they don't respond after 8 weeks (Steroid-resistant), then we do the Renal Biopsy.

quiz.q: What is the most common cause of nephrotic syndrome in children?

title: 🧂 ADRENAL INSUFFICIENCY (ADDISON'S)
content:
FeaturePrimary (Addison's)Secondary (Pituitary)
SkinHyperpigmentationPale skin
AldosteroneDeficient (Low Na/High K)Normal
ACTH LevelHighLow

⚠️ Exam Trap: Hyperpigmentation (bronzing) only occurs in Primary adrenal insufficiency because high ACTH cross-reacts with melanin receptors.

quiz.q: What is the screening test for Adrenal Insufficiency?
quiz.a: Standard-dose (250 mcg) ACTH Stimulation Test (Cosyntropin test).

title: 🔪 ACUTE MESENTERIC ISCHEMIA
content:
The 'Heart Attack' of the bowel. Usually due to AFib or Embolism.

• Classic Buzzword: "Pain out of proportion" to physical exam findings (Abdomen is soft but patient is screaming).
• Dx: CT Angiography.

⚠️ Exam Trap: If the question mentions Metabolic Acidosis and high Lactate, the bowel is already dying (Infarction). This is a surgical emergency!

quiz.q: What cardiac condition is frequently associated with mesenteric embolism?

title: ⚡ HYPERCALCEMIA MANAGEMENT
content:
"Stones, Bones, Groans, and Psychiatric Overtones".

Management Steps:
1. Aggressive IV Fluids (Normal Saline) - Always the first step!
2. Bisphosphonates (Pamidronate/Zoledronate).
3. Calcitonin (for rapid, short-term effect).

⚠️ Exam Trap: Avoid Thiazide diuretics in hypercalcemia because they increase calcium reabsorption! Use Loop diuretics (Furosemide) only AFTER the patient is rehydrated.

quiz.q: What is the most common cause of hypercalcemia in hospitalized patients?

title: 🚫 SUICIDE RISK ASSESSMENT
content:
A mandatory check for every psychiatric presentation in Canada.

Risk Factors (SAD PERSONS):
• S: Sex (Male).
• A: Age (Young/Elderly).
• D: Depression.
• P: Previous Attempt (#1 Factor).
• E: Ethanol/Drugs.
• R: Rational loss.
• S: Sickness (Chronic).

⚠️ Exam Trap: If a patient has an Active Plan and Intent, they cannot leave the ED. If they refuse, you must Form them (Involuntary Admission) for their safety. Privacy is secondary to life!

quiz.q: What is the single strongest predictor of a completed suicide?
quiz.a: A history of previous suicide attempts.

title: 🦵 PERTHES VS SCFE (HIP PAIN)
content:
Sorting the limping child.

FeatureLegg-Calve-PerthesSlipped Capital (SCFE)
Age4 - 10 years10 - 16 years
WeightNormal/ThinObese / Overweight
PathologyAvascular NecrosisGrowth Plate Slip

SVG Visual: SCFE Slip

⚠️ Exam Trap: Hip pathology often presents as isolated knee pain (Referred pain). If an obese 13-year-old complains of knee pain, the first step is a Hip X-ray (AP and Frog-leg).

quiz.q: What is the initial management for a confirmed SCFE?
quiz.a: Non-weight bearing (Crutches) and immediate Surgical consultation for pinning.

title: 🫀 INFECTIVE ENDOCARDITIS (DUKE)
content:
Diagnosis requires 2 Major OR 1 Major + 3 Minor criteria.

• Major: 2+ Positive blood cultures (Typical organisms), ECHO showing vegetation.
• Minor: Fever, IV drug use, Janeway lesions (painless), Osler nodes (painful).

⚠️ Exam Trap: If endocarditis is suspected, you must draw 3 sets of blood cultures from different sites BEFORE starting any antibiotics!

quiz.q: What is the most common organism in subacute endocarditis on a damaged valve?

title: 👶 BIOPHYSICAL PROFILE (BPP)
content:
Assessing fetal well-being when NST is non-reactive.

5 Components (2 points each):
1. NST: Reactive.
2. Breathing: ≥ 1 episode (>30 sec).
3. Movement: ≥ 3 discrete body movements.
4. Tone: ≥ 1 episode of extension/flexion.
5. AFV: At least one pocket 2x2 cm.

⚠️ Exam Trap: A score of 8/10 or 10/10 is normal. A score of 4/10 or less indicates fetal distress and usually requires Urgent Delivery!

quiz.q: What does a low amniotic fluid volume (AFV) usually indicate in the BPP?

title: ⚖️ FITNESS TO DRIVE (CANADA)
content:
Physicians have a legal duty to report patients who are unfit to drive due to medical conditions.

• Conditions: Uncontrolled Epilepsy, Severe Dementia, Substance Abuse, Sleep Apnea (with daytime sleepiness).
• Who to report: The Provincial Ministry of Transportation (e.g., MTO in Ontario).

⚠️ Exam Trap: You do not need patient consent to report. Public safety overrides patient confidentiality in this scenario. You should inform the patient that you are reporting them, but you must proceed regardless of their opinion.

quiz.q: How long must a patient be seizure-free before they can usually return to driving in most provinces?

title: 🦋 ANTITHYROID DRUGS SIDE EFFECTS
content:
Methimazole and PTU (Propylthiouracil).

• Most Dangerous: Agranulocytosis (Sudden drop in WBCs).
• Symptoms: Fever, Sore throat, Mouth ulcers.

⚠️ Exam Trap: If a patient on Methimazole develops a fever or sore throat, the next step is IMMEDIATE CBC and stopping the drug. Do not wait for symptoms to worsen!

quiz.q: Which antithyroid drug is preferred in the FIRST trimester of pregnancy?
quiz.a: PTU (due to lower risk of teratogenicity/aplasia cutis).

title: 🧱 DIVERTICULITIS MANAGEMENT
content:
Left Lower Quadrant (LLQ) pain + Fever.

• Dx: CT Scan with contrast (shows fat stranding/thickening).
• Tx (Uncomplicated): Bowel rest + Oral Antibiotics (Cipro/Metronidazole).
• Tx (Complicated/Abscess): IV Abx + Percutaneous drainage.

⚠️ Exam Trap: Avoid Colonoscopy or Barium Enema during the acute phase of diverticulitis because of the high risk of Perforation. Wait 6 weeks!

quiz.q: What is the most common complication of diverticulitis?

title: 🧂 HYPERNATREMIA CORRECTION
content:
Sodium > 145 mmol/L (usually due to dehydration).

• Risk: Correcting too fast causes Cerebral Edema.
• Rate: Do not exceed 0.5 mmol/L per hour (Max 10-12 mmol/day).

⚠️ Exam Trap: Use D5W or 0.45% Saline (Hypotonic fluids) to correct hypernatremia. If the patient is hypovolemic (Low BP), give Normal Saline (0.9%) first to stabilize vitals, then switch to hypotonic.

quiz.q: What is the main danger of correcting chronic hypernatremia too rapidly?

title: 🧠 LITHIUM VS VALPROATE
content:
First-line mood stabilizers.

• Lithium: Best for suicidal ideation/classic mania. (Risk: Thyroid/Renal).
• Valproate: Best for rapid cycling/mixed episodes. (Risk: Liver/PCOS).

⚠️ Exam Trap: Valproate is the most teratogenic (Neural Tube Defects). In a young woman of child-bearing age with Bipolar, Lithium or Lamotrigine are generally safer choices than Valproate.

quiz.q: What test must be performed before starting a patient on Lithium?
quiz.a: Creatinine (Renal function), TSH (Thyroid), and Pregnancy test.

title: 💓 PATHOLOGIC MURMURS IN KIDS
content:
How to spot a 'Bad' murmur.

Innocent (Benign)Pathologic (Abnormal)
Soft / Grade < 3Loud / Grade ≥ 3
Vibratory / MusicalHarsh / Blowing
Varies with positionFixed / Does not change

⚠️ Exam Trap: All Diastolic murmurs and all Holosystolic murmurs in children are PATHOLOGIC until proven otherwise. They require an urgent ECHO.

quiz.q: Is a grade 4 systolic murmur in a 5-year-old considered innocent?
quiz.a: No, grade 3 or higher is a red flag for pathology.

title: 💩 COLORECTAL CANCER SCREENING
content:
Canadian Task Force Guidelines (Average Risk).

• Who: Adults aged 50 to 74.
• Test: FIT or gFOBT every 2 years.
• Alternative: Flexible Sigmoidoscopy every 10 years.

⚠️ Exam Trap: If a 1st-degree relative was diagnosed < 60 years old, screening starts at Age 40 (or 10 years before diagnosis) and the test MUST be Colonoscopy every 5 years, not FIT!

quiz.q: What is the preferred screening test for average-risk individuals in Canada?

title: 🎀 BREAST CANCER SCREENING
content:
Current Canadian Guidelines (Average Risk).

• Age 50 - 74: Mammogram every 2 years.
• Age 40 - 49: Not routinely recommended (Shared decision making).

SVG Visual: Mammogram

⚠️ Exam Trap: Clinical Breast Exam (CBE) and Breast Self-Exam (BSE) are NOT recommended for screening in asymptomatic women in Canada (low evidence).

quiz.q: At what age does routine mammography screening stop for average-risk women in Canada?

title: 🍓 KAWASAKI DISEASE
content:
Medium-vessel vasculitis. Think CRASH and Burn.

• C: Conjunctivitis (Non-exudative).
• R: Rash (Polymorphous).
• A: Adenopathy (Cervical).
• S: Strawberry Tongue / Oral changes.
• H: Hand/Foot edema/desquamation.
• Burn: Fever ≥ 5 days.

⚠️ Exam Trap: The most feared complication is Coronary Artery Aneurysm. Initial treatment is IVIG + High-dose Aspirin. (Note: This is one of the few times Aspirin is okay in kids!).

quiz.q: What imaging must be done once Kawasaki is suspected?

title: 🦠 TUBERCULOSIS (TST RULES)
content:
Tuberculin Skin Test (Mantoux) interpretation.

IndurationPositive if...
≥ 5 mmHIV+, Recent contact with TB, Fibrotic CXR.
≥ 10 mmHigh-risk countries, IV drug users, Kids < 4.
≥ 15 mmNo known risk factors.

⚠️ Exam Trap: A previous BCG Vaccine can cause a false positive TST. In these cases, IGRA (blood test) is preferred because it is not affected by BCG.

quiz.q: How long after the injection should the TST be read?

title: 🤝 INTIMATE PARTNER VIOLENCE (IPV)
content:
Physician role in suspected domestic abuse.

• Approach: Ask directly, validated, and non-judgmental.
• Safety: Assess immediate danger and have a 'Safety Plan'.

⚠️ Exam Trap: Unlike child abuse, reporting IPV for competent adults is NOT mandatory in Canada without their consent (Privacy vs. Autonomy). EXCEPTION: If children are in the home and witnessing abuse, you MUST report to Child Services!

quiz.q: What is the priority when a patient discloses IPV?
quiz.a: Assessment of immediate safety and providing resources/safety plan.

title: 🦶 DIABETIC FOOT ULCER (WAGNER)
content:
Leading cause of non-traumatic amputations.

• Pathophysiology: Neuropathy + Ischemia + Trauma.
• Screening: Annual 10g Monofilament test.

SVG Visual: Ulcer Depth

⚠️ Exam Trap: If an ulcer probes to Bone (Probe-to-bone test), the diagnosis is Osteomyelitis until proven otherwise. Next step: MRI or Bone Biopsy.

quiz.q: Which type of neuropathy is most common in Diabetic foot?

title: 🚬 SMOKING CESSATION (CANADIAN OPS)
content:
Pharmacotherapy options in Canada.

• 1st Line: Varenicline (Chantix), Bupropion (Zyban), or NRT (Patch/Gum).
• Combination: Patch + Gum/Inhaler is more effective than either alone.

⚠️ Exam Trap: Varenicline is generally the most effective single agent. Bupropion is a great choice if the patient also has Depression, but avoid it if there is a History of Seizures or Eating Disorders!

quiz.q: What is a major contraindication for Bupropion?
quiz.a: Seizure disorder or Bulimia/Anorexia (lowers seizure threshold).

title: 💉 HIV PEP VS PREP
content:
FeaturePrEP (Pre-Exposure)PEP (Post-Exposure)
IndicationHigh-risk lifestyleAccidental exposure (Needle/Sex)
TimingContinuousWithin 72 hours
DurationOngoing28 Days

⚠️ Exam Trap: If a healthcare worker has a needle-stick injury, PEP must be started as soon as possible (ideally within 2 hours). Do not wait for the source patient's blood results to start the first dose!

quiz.q: What is the maximum time window to start PEP after exposure?

title: ⚖️ ADVANCE DIRECTIVES (SDM)
content:
Hierarchy of Substitute Decision Makers (Ontario example).

1. Guardian (appointed by court).
2. Attorney for personal care.
3. Representative (appointed by CCB).
4. Spouse or Partner.
5. Children / Parents.

⚠️ Exam Trap: An SDM must follow the patient's Known Wishes. If wishes are unknown, they must act in the patient's Best Interest. A spouse cannot override a patient's previously written 'Living Will'.

quiz.q: Who is the default SDM for an unmarried patient with no children?

title: 👶 18-MONTH WELL BABY VISIT
content:
A critical milestone in the Canadian Rourke Baby Record.

• Motor: Walks well, runs, climbs stairs with help.
• Language: 15 - 20 words, points to body parts.
• Social: Symbolic play (feeding a doll).

⚠️ Exam Trap: Not walking by 18 months is a major developmental red flag and requires immediate investigation for GDD or neuromuscular issues.

quiz.q: What is the standard tool used in Canada to screen for Autism at 18 months?
quiz.a: The M-CHAT-R (Modified Checklist for Autism in Toddlers).

title: 🤰 PRENATAL SCREENING (ANEUPLOIDY)
content:
Canadian SOGC guidelines for Down Syndrome screening.

• NIPT (Cell-free DNA): Most sensitive (>99%). Used for high-risk patients.
• IPS (Integrated): Ultrasound (NT) + Blood tests.

FindingCondition
High NT / Low PAPP-AIncreased risk of Trisomy 21
High β-hCG / Low AFPIncreased risk of Trisomy 21


⚠️ Exam Trap: Screening tests (NIPT/IPS) only give probabilities. If a screening test is positive, the next step is Invasive Testing (CVS or Amniocentesis) for confirmation.

quiz.q: What is the most sensitive non-invasive screening test for Trisomy 21?

title: 🫀 SYSTOLIC VS DIASTOLIC MURMURS
content:
A crucial distinction for the clinical exam.

• Systolic: AS, MR, PS, TR (Can be innocent).
• Diastolic: AR, MS, PR, TS (Always Pathologic).


⚠️ Exam Trap: All Diastolic murmurs require an Echocardiogram. A soft systolic murmur (Grade 1-2) in a young, asymptomatic patient is often just observed.

quiz.q: Is a diastolic murmur ever considered 'innocent' in a child?
quiz.a: No, diastolic murmurs are always pathologic.

title: 🧠 DELIRIUM WORKUP (DEMENTIA MNEMONIC)
content:
Causes of acute confusion in the elderly.

D: Drugs (Benzos/Anticholinergics).
E: Electrolytes (Low Na/High Ca).
M: Metabolic (Hypoxia/Glucose).
I: Infection (UTI/Pneumonia).
N: Nutrition (B12 deficiency).

⚠️ Exam Trap: Avoid using Benzodiazepines to treat delirium (unless it's alcohol withdrawal) because they worsen the confusion and increase fall risk!

quiz.q: What is the most common cause of delirium in an elderly patient with no fever?
quiz.a: Urinary Tract Infection (UTI) or medication side effects.

title: 🤰 UTI IN PREGNANCY
content:
Asymptomatic Bacteriuria (ASB) must be treated in pregnancy.

• Threshold: ≥ 10^5 CFU/mL in an asymptomatic patient.
• Why: High risk of progression to Pyelonephritis and Preterm labor.
• Tx: Nitrofurantoin or Amoxicillin (Avoid Nitro in late 3rd trimester).

⚠️ Exam Trap: In the general population, we don't treat ASB. In pregnancy and before urological surgery, we ALWAYS treat it.

quiz.q: Why is Nitrofurantoin avoided at term (near delivery)?
quiz.a: Risk of hemolytic anemia (G6PD related) in the neonate.

title: 🧷 OCP CONTRAINDICATIONS
content:
When to avoid Combined Oral Contraceptives (Estrogen).

• Age > 35 + Smoker (>15 cig/day).
• History of VTE/PE.
• Migraine with Aura.
• Breast Cancer.

⚠️ Exam Trap: If a woman has Migraine with Aura, estrogen-containing pills increase the risk of Ischemic Stroke significantly. Use Progestin-only pills or an IUD instead.

quiz.q: Can a breastfeeding woman take combined OCPs early postpartum?
quiz.a: No, Estrogen can decrease milk production; Progestin-only is preferred.

title: 🦴 OSTEOPOROSIS (FRAX SCORE)
content:
Screening for bone health in Canada.

• Who: All adults ≥ 65 years (DEXA scan).
• High Risk: Glucocorticoid use, prior fracture, Rheumatoid Arthritis.
• Tx: Bisphosphonates (Alendronate) + Vitamin D/Calcium.

[attachment_0](attachment)
⚠️ Exam Trap: Patients on chronic Steroids (≥ 3 months) should be considered for treatment earlier, regardless of age.

quiz.q: How should Bisphosphonates be taken to avoid esophagitis?
quiz.a: First thing in the morning, on an empty stomach, with a full glass of water, and staying upright for 30 minutes.

title: 🧠 PARKINSON'S DISEASE
content:
Clinical diagnosis based on the 'TRAP' mnemonic.

T: Tremor (Resting, 'Pill-rolling').
R: Rigidity (Cogwheel).
A: Akinesia / Bradykinesia.
P: Postural Instability.

⚠️ Exam Trap: Parkinsonism can be induced by Antipsychotics (D2 blockers). If the symptoms are Bilateral and Symmetric from the start, suspect drug-induced rather than idiopathic PD.

quiz.q: What is the most effective medication for Parkinson's symptoms?

title: 👶 FIFTH DISEASE (PARVOVIRUS B19)
content:
Erythema Infectiosum.

• Signs: 'Slapped-cheek' rash + Lacy reticular rash on body.
• Concern: Aplastic crisis in Sickle Cell patients.
• Pregnancy: Risk of Hydrops Fetalis.


⚠️ Exam Trap: Once the rash appears, the child is no longer contagious and can return to school/daycare.

quiz.q: What is the primary complication of Parvovirus B19 in a pregnant woman?
quiz.a: Fetal hydrops (due to severe fetal anemia).

title: ⚖️ DUTY TO WARN (TARASOFF)
content:
When can you break confidentiality to protect others?

• Requirement: Serious, imminent risk of harm to an identifiable person or group.
• Action: You must warn the police and the potential victim.

⚠️ Exam Trap: If the threat is vague (e.g., "I want to hurt someone"), you explore the risk. If it is specific (e.g., "I am going to kill my neighbor John tonight"), you MUST report.

quiz.q: Does a physician have a duty to report a patient who is HIV+ but engaging in unprotected sex without disclosing it?
quiz.a: In Canada, you should first encourage the patient, but you may have a duty to report to Public Health or warn the partner if the risk is high.

title: 🦠 SEPSIS BUNDLE (1-HOUR)
content:
Rapid management is the key to survival.

1. Measure Lactate level.
2. Obtain blood cultures (before antibiotics).
3. Administer Broad-spectrum Antibiotics.
4. Start IV Fluid resuscitation (30mL/kg).

⚠️ Exam Trap: Do not wait for imaging or lab results if the patient is in Septic Shock. Fluids and Antibiotics are the priority.

quiz.q: What is the goal MAP (Mean Arterial Pressure) in sepsis resuscitation?

title: 🩸 DYSMENORRHEA: PRIMARY VS SECONDARY
content:
A very common Canadian exam topic to differentiate causes of pelvic pain.

FeaturePrimarySecondary
Onset1-2 years after menarcheYears after menarche (>25y)
PathologyExcess ProstaglandinsEndometriosis / Fibroids
Pain timingStarts with mensesStarts before menses

⚠️ Exam Trap: If the patient has Dyspareunia (painful sex) or Infertility, the answer is almost always Endometriosis (Secondary), not primary dysmenorrhea.

quiz.q: What is the first-line medical treatment for primary dysmenorrhea?

title: 🎗️ ENDOMETRIOSIS (THE 3 D'S)
content:
Endometrial tissue outside the uterine cavity. High yield for MCCQE1.

The Classic Triad (3 D's):
1. Dysmenorrhea (Painful periods).
2. Dyspareunia (Painful intercourse).
3. Dyschezia (Painful defecation during menses).


⚠️ Exam Trap: Laparoscopy with biopsy is the ONLY way to definitively diagnose endometriosis. Ultrasound is often normal unless there is an Endometrioma (Chocolate cyst).

quiz.q: What is the gold standard for definitive diagnosis of endometriosis?

title: 🌡️ NEONATAL FEVER (< 28 DAYS)
content:
Medical Emergency! Any fever in a neonate is sepsis until proven otherwise.

The Full Sepsis Workup:
• Blood Cultures.
• Urine Culture (via catheter).
• Lumbar Puncture (CSF).
• Chest X-ray (if respiratory signs).


⚠️ Exam Trap: You must Admit the neonate and start IV Ampicillin + Gentamicin (or Cefotaxime) immediately after cultures. Do not wait for results!

quiz.q: Why is Ceftriaxone avoided in neonates?
quiz.a: Risk of biliary sludging and displacing bilirubin (Kernicterus).

title: ⚡ ATRIAL FIBRILLATION: RATE VS RHYTHM
content:
Management based on Canadian CCS Guidelines.

• Rate Control: Preferred for most (Beta-blockers or CCBs).
• Rhythm Control: For young patients, highly symptomatic, or heart failure.

⚠️ Exam Trap: Regardless of rate or rhythm choice, the most important decision is Anticoagulation (CHADS-65). Rhythm control does NOT eliminate the need for anticoagulants!

quiz.q: When is electrical cardioversion the immediate first step in AFib?
quiz.a: When the patient is hemodynamically unstable (e.g., hypotension, pulmonary edema).

title: 🦠 ERYSIPELAS VS CELLULITIS
content:
Distinguishing skin infections is common in the ED.

FeatureErysipelasCellulitis
LayerSuperficial DermisDeep Dermis/Subcutis
EdgesSharply DemarcatedIll-defined / Blurry
OrganismGroup A StrepS. aureus / Strep


⚠️ Exam Trap: Erysipelas often presents on the face or lower limbs with a bright red, raised appearance. Cellulitis is deeper and lacks the raised border.

quiz.q: What is the classic description of erysipelas edges?

title: 🧠 BIPOLAR I VS BIPOLAR II
content:
Classification based on the severity of the 'High'.

• Bipolar I: At least one Manic episode (≥ 7 days or requires hospitalization).
• Bipolar II: Hypomanic episode (≥ 4 days, no hospitalization) PLUS a Major Depressive episode.

⚠️ Exam Trap: If there is ANY history of Psychosis during a high mood, it is automatically Bipolar I (Mania), even if it lasted only 2 days.

quiz.q: What is the core difference between Mania and Hypomania regarding function?
quiz.a: Mania causes marked impairment in social/occupational functioning or requires hospitalization.

title: 👁️ CENTRAL RETINAL ARTERY OCCLUSION (CRAO)
content:
An ocular emergency (Stroke of the eye).

• Symptoms: Sudden, Painless, unilateral vision loss.
• Fundoscopy: Cherry-red spot on a pale macula.


⚠️ Exam Trap: If the vision loss is painful, think Acute Glaucoma. If there are flashes and floaters before a 'curtain' falls, think Retinal Detachment.

quiz.q: What classic fundoscopy finding is seen in CRAO?
quiz.a: A cherry-red spot on the macula.

title: 🌬️ COPD: ACUTE EXACERBATION
content:
When to give antibiotics in COPD (Anthonisen Criteria).

Give Antibiotics if 2/3 are present:
1. Increased Dyspnea.
2. Increased Sputum Volume.
3. Increased Sputum Purulence (Yellow/Green).

⚠️ Exam Trap: For the exam, Purulence is the most important cardinal symptom for starting antibiotics. Treatment is usually 5 days.

quiz.q: What are the first-line bronchodilators for an acute COPD exacerbation?

title: 🎄 PITYRIASIS ROSEA
content:
Benign skin rash, possibly viral (HHV-6/7).

• Stage 1: Herald Patch (Large, oval, scaly patch).
• Stage 2: Christmas Tree distribution (smaller lesions along skin lines).


⚠️ Exam Trap: This rash is Self-limiting (6-8 weeks). No treatment is needed unless it's very itchy (then use topical steroids). Do not confuse with secondary syphilis!

quiz.q: What is the initial lesion called in pityriasis rosea?

title: 🧠 BRAIN ABSCESS
content:
Focal infection in the brain parenchyma.

• Triad: Headache + Fever + Focal Neurologic Deficit.
• Imaging: Ring-enhancing lesion on CT/MRI with contrast.

⚠️ Exam Trap: Lumbar Puncture is CONTRAINDICATED if a brain abscess is suspected due to the risk of Brain Herniation from increased ICP.

quiz.q: What is the classic appearance of a brain abscess on CT scan with contrast?

title: ⚽ ACUTE SCROTUM: TORSION VS EPIDIDYMITIS
content:
A critical surgical distinction to save the testis.

FeatureTesticular TorsionEpididymitis
AgePuberty (12-18y)Post-pubertal (>18y)
OnsetSudden / SevereGradual
Cremasteric ReflexAbsentPresent
Phren's SignNo relief with elevationRelief with elevation

[attachment_0](attachment)
⚠️ Exam Trap: In MCCQE1, if you suspect Torsion, the next best step is Immediate Surgical Exploration. Do not delay surgery for an Ultrasound if the clinical suspicion is high!

quiz.q: What is the definitive 'gold standard' for diagnosing testicular torsion?
quiz.a: Surgical exploration (Ultrasound is helpful but should not delay surgery).

title: 💧 PEDIATRIC REHYDRATION (4-2-1 RULE)
content:
Assessing and treating dehydration in children.

Maintenance Fluids (4-2-1 Rule):
• First 10 kg: 4 mL/kg/hr.
• Next 10 kg: 2 mL/kg/hr.
• Each kg after: 1 mL/kg/hr.

⚠️ Exam Trap: For Mild-Moderate dehydration, the first-line treatment is Oral Rehydration Therapy (ORT). IV fluids are reserved only for Severe dehydration (shock) or failure of ORT.

quiz.q: What is the best objective measure of a child's dehydration level?
quiz.a: Percentage of body weight lost (if baseline weight is known).

title: 🩸 ABNORMAL UTERINE BLEEDING (PALM-COEIN)
content:
Systematic classification of AUB causes.

Structural (PALM):
• P: Polyp.
• A: Adenomyosis.
• L: Leiomyoma (Fibroids).
• M: Malignancy.

Non-Structural (COEIN):
• C: Coagulopathy.
• O: Ovulatory dysfunction.
• E: Endometrial.
• I: Iatrogenic.
• N: Not classified.

⚠️ Exam Trap: In any woman > 40 years (or > 35 with risk factors) with AUB, the mandatory next step is an Endometrial Biopsy to rule out cancer.

quiz.q: What is the most common cause of AUB in adolescent girls?

title: 💉 Rh ISOIMMUNIZATION (WinRho)
content:
Preventing hemolytic disease of the newborn.

• Who: Rh-negative mothers with Rh-positive fetuses.
• When: Give Anti-D (WinRho) at 28 weeks and within 72 hours of delivery.
• Other times: Miscarriage, trauma, amniocentesis.

⚠️ Exam Trap: You do NOT give WinRho if the mother is already Sensitized (Antibody titer > 0). If the titer is 1:16 or higher, fetal monitoring with MCA Doppler is required.

quiz.q: What test is used to determine the exact dose of WinRho needed after a major trauma?

title: 🍺 ALCOHOL WITHDRAWAL (DTs)
content:
A life-threatening medical emergency.

TimeSymptoms
6-24hTremor, Anxiety, Tachycardia.
12-48hSeizures (Withdrawal seizures).
48-96hDelirium Tremens (DTs): Confusion, Hallucinations, Fever.

⚠️ Exam Trap: DTs have a mortality rate of 5%. The gold standard treatment is Benzodiazepines (Diazepam/Lorazepam) given based on the CIWA score.

quiz.q: What is the primary pharmacological treatment for severe alcohol withdrawal?

title: 🧪 CHOLINERGIC VS ANTICHOLINERGIC
content:
FeatureCholinergic (OP)Anticholinergic (Atropine)
PupilsPinpoint (Miosis)Dilated (Mydriasis)
SkinSweaty (Diaphoretic)Dry / Red / Hot
BowelDiarrheaConstipation / Ileus

⚠️ Exam Trap: Remember: "Dry as a bone, Red as a beet, Mad as a hatter" for Anticholinergic. For OP poisoning (Cholinergic), use Atropine as the antidote.

quiz.q: What is the mnemonic for cholinergic toxicity symptoms?
quiz.a: DUMBELS (Diarrhea, Urination, Miosis, Bradycardia, Emesis, Lacrimation, Salivation).

title: ⚖️ DISCLOSURE OF ADVERSE EVENTS
content:
Legal and ethical duty in Canada (CMPA rules).

• What to disclose: Any event that caused harm to the patient.
• Apology: In most provinces, an apology is NOT an admission of legal liability.
• Timing: As soon as possible after the event is recognized.

⚠️ Exam Trap: If an error occurred but caused NO harm (a 'near miss'), disclosure is not legally mandatory but often ethically encouraged to improve safety.

quiz.q: Does a physician have to explain exactly how an error happened during disclosure?
quiz.a: Yes, they should provide the facts of what happened without speculating on legal blame.

title: 🎒 HERNIAS: INCARCERATED VS STRANGULATED
content:
Definitions:
• Incarcerated: Hernia cannot be reduced (pushed back).
• Strangulated: Blood supply is cut off (Ischemia).

⚠️ Exam Trap: A Strangulated hernia is a surgical emergency. Look for Fever, High WBC, and extreme tenderness. Never try to reduce a suspected strangulated hernia!

quiz.q: What is the most common type of groin hernia?

title: 🤒 MEASLES (RUBEOLA)
content:
Highly contagious viral infection.

The 3 C's:
• C: Cough.
• C: Coryza (Runny nose).
• C: Conjunctivitis.


⚠️ Exam Trap: The rash in Measles starts on the face/hairline and spreads downwards (Cephalocaudal spread). Koplik spots on the buccal mucosa are pathognomonic.

quiz.q: What vitamin supplementation is proven to reduce mortality in children with severe measles?

title: 👶 PRETERM LABOR MANAGEMENT
content:
Labor before 37 weeks gestation.

Key Medications:
• Steroids (Betamethasone): For fetal lung maturity (<34 weeks).
• Tocolytics (Nifedipine/Indomethacin): To delay labor for 48 hours.
• Magnesium Sulfate: For Fetal Neuroprotection (<32 weeks).

⚠️ Exam Trap: Magnesium sulfate in preterm labor is used for fetal brain protection (cerebral palsy prevention), NOT to stop the contractions (Tocolysis).

quiz.q: Up to what gestational age is Magnesium Sulfate indicated for fetal neuroprotection?
quiz.a: Up to 31 weeks and 6 days (32 weeks).

title: 🧠 CLUSTER B PERSONALITY DISORDERS
content:
Canadian exams focus on the 'Dramatic' cluster and their impact on the doctor-patient relationship.

TypeKey FeatureManagement Goal
BorderlineFear of abandonment, 'Splitting' (idealizing then devaluing)Clear boundaries (Firmness).
AntisocialDisregard for rules, lack of remorseSet firm limits on drug-seeking.
NarcissisticGrandiosity, lacks empathyAvoid power struggles.
HistrionicAttention-seeking, seductive behaviorMaintain professional distance.

⚠️ Exam Trap: If a patient tries to 'split' the medical team (praising you while insulting the nurse), the answer is Joint Team Meetings to ensure a consistent message and clear boundaries.

quiz.q: What is the primary defense mechanism used by patients with Borderline Personality Disorder?

title: 🦵 DEVELOPMENTAL DYSPLASIA OF THE HIP (DDH)
content:
Abnormal development of the hip joint. Screening is mandatory at every visit until 1 year.

• Screening: Barlow (dislocates) and Ortolani (reduces) maneuvers.
• Imaging: Ultrasound if < 6 months; X-ray if > 6 months.


⚠️ Exam Trap: If the child is older (>3 months), Barlow/Ortolani may be negative. Look for Limited Abduction or asymmetric skin folds. The classic treatment is the Pavlik Harness.

quiz.q: Up to what age is Ultrasound the preferred imaging for DDH?
quiz.a: Under 6 months of age (before the femoral head ossifies).

title: 🎗️ POLYCYSTIC OVARY SYNDROME (ROTTERDAM)
content:
Diagnosis requires 2 of 3 criteria:
1. Oligo/Anovulation (Irregular menses).
2. Hyperandrogenism (Hirsutism/Acne/High Testosterone).
3. Polycystic ovaries on ultrasound.

⚠️ Exam Trap: PCOS increases the risk of Endometrial Cancer due to unopposed estrogen. Treatment for infertility is Letrozole (first-line in Canada over Clomiphene).

quiz.q: What is the first-line treatment for PCOS-related hirsutism?

title: 🤰 PLACENTA PREVIA VS ABRUPTION
content:
Comparing the two 'Big Killers' of late pregnancy bleeding.

FeaturePlacenta PreviaPlacental Abruption
PainPainlessSevere Abdominal Pain
Vaginal BleedBright RedDark / Concealed
UterusSoft / Non-tenderTender / Woody Hard


⚠️ Exam Trap: NEVER do a digital vaginal exam until Previa is ruled out by ultrasound. It can cause massive, fatal hemorrhage!

quiz.q: What is a major risk factor for placental abruption?
quiz.a: Hypertension (most common), Cocaine use, or Trauma.

title: 🎀 BREAST LUMP WORKUP (TRIPLE ASSESSMENT)
content:
Systematic approach for any palpable breast mass.

1. Clinical Exam.
2. Imaging: Ultrasound if < 35y; Mammogram if > 35y.
3. Pathology: Core Needle Biopsy (preferred over FNA).

⚠️ Exam Trap: A 'Simple Cyst' on ultrasound that is asymptomatic does not need intervention. If it's painful, aspirate it. If the fluid is Bloody, it MUST go for cytology.

quiz.q: What is the preferred biopsy method for a suspicious breast mass?

title: 🌬️ TENSION PNEUMOTHORAX
content:
Life-Threatening Emergency! Air enters pleural space but cannot exit, compressing the heart.

• Signs: Tracheal deviation (to opposite side), Absent breath sounds, Hypotension (Shock).

⚠️ Exam Trap: This is a Clinical Diagnosis. If you see 'Tracheal deviation' and 'Shock', do NOT wait for an X-ray. Perform Immediate Needle Decompression (2nd ICS, MCL or 5th ICS, MAL).

quiz.q: Where is the recommended site for needle decompression in Canada (ATLS 10)?
quiz.a: 4th or 5th intercostal space at the mid-axillary line.

title: ⚖️ CONTRACEPTION & THE MINOR
content:
Consent for sexual health in Canada.

• Confidentiality: Minors can receive contraception and STI testing without parental consent if they are 'mature'.
• Physician Duty: You do not have to inform parents even if the minor is 13-14, as long as there is no abuse suspected.

⚠️ Exam Trap: If a minor asks for contraception, your first step is to Assess Capacity (ability to understand risks/benefits). If they are capable, you provide it. Parents' wishes cannot override this.

quiz.q: Does a physician need parental consent to prescribe the OCP to a 15-year-old with capacity?

title: 🍼 PYLORIC STENOSIS (INFANT)
content:
Non-bilious, projectile vomiting in an infant (3-6 weeks).

• Physical: 'Olive-shaped' mass in RUQ.
• Labs: Hypochloremic, Hypokalemic Metabolic Alkalosis.

⚠️ Exam Trap: The most important First Step is NOT surgery (Pyloromyotomy). It is Fluid Resuscitation to correct the metabolic alkalosis first! Surgery is elective once stable.

quiz.q: What is the classic metabolic abnormality in pyloric stenosis?

title: 🩸 ACUTE MESENTERIC ISCHEMIA
content:
Compromised blood flow to the small bowel. High mortality!

• History: Patient with AFib or Atherosclerosis.
• Key Sign: 'Pain out of proportion' to physical exam (soft belly but screaming patient).

⚠️ Exam Trap: Don't wait for 'Peritonitis' signs. If a patient with AFib has severe sudden abdominal pain, the answer is CT Angiography immediately.

quiz.q: What is the imaging of choice for suspected mesenteric ischemia?

title: 🧠 SCHIZOPHRENIA: NEGATIVE SYMPTOMS
content:
Negative symptoms are often more disabling than positive ones (hallucinations).

The 5 A's:
1. Alogia (Poverty of speech).
2. Affective flattening.
3. Avolition (Lack of motivation).
4. Anhedonia.
5. Attention impairment.

⚠️ Exam Trap: Typical antipsychotics (Haloperidol) improve positive symptoms but may worsen negative ones. Atypical antipsychotics (e.g., Clozapine, Risperidone) are better for the overall picture.

quiz.q: Which medication is the 'Gold Standard' for treatment-resistant schizophrenia?

title: 💊 OPIOID OVERDOSE & NALOXONE
content:
A massive public health issue in Canada. Recognising the triad is life-saving.

The Classic Triad:
1. Miosis (Pinpoint pupils).
2. Respiratory Depression (RR < 12).
3. CNS Depression (Coma/Unresponsiveness).

⚠️ Exam Trap: In Canada, Naloxone can be given IM, IV, or Intranasally. If the patient is not breathing, Ventilation (BVM) is the first priority while preparing Naloxone. Do not wait for the pulse to start Naloxone!

quiz.q: What is the primary cause of death in opioid overdose?

title: 🦠 PERTUSSIS (WHOOPING COUGH)
content:
Caused by Bordetella pertussis. Highly tested in Canadian pediatric stems.

Stages:
1. Catarrhal: URI symptoms (Most contagious).
2. Paroxysmal: Whooping cough, Post-tussive emesis (Classic clue).
3. Convalescent: Gradual recovery.

⚠️ Exam Trap: All close contacts (regardless of immunization status) must receive Post-exposure Prophylaxis with Macrolides (Azithromycin) to prevent spread.

quiz.q: What is the characteristic lab finding in pertussis?
quiz.a: Absolute Lymphocytosis (High WBC with high lymphocytes).

title: 🍂 MENOPAUSE & HRT RULES
content:
12 months of amenorrhea. Management of symptoms (Hot flashes).

Patient StatusRecommended HRT
Has UterusEstrogen + Progestin
No Uterus (Post-hysterectomy)Estrogen Alone

⚠️ Exam Trap: Never give Estrogen alone to a woman with a uterus; it causes Endometrial Hyperplasia/Cancer. Progestin is mandatory for 'Endometrial Protection'.

quiz.q: What is the most effective treatment for moderate-to-severe vasomotor symptoms (hot flashes)?

title: 🟡 ACUTE CHOLANGITIS (REYNOLDS')
content:
Biliary obstruction with superimposed infection. A true surgical emergency!

• Charcot's Triad: RUQ Pain + Fever + Jaundice.
• Reynolds' Pentad: Triad + Hypotension + Altered Mental Status.

⚠️ Exam Trap: Treatment is EMERGENCY Biliary Decompression (usually via ERCP) plus IV antibiotics. Do not wait for the patient to 'stabilize' if they have the Pentad.

quiz.q: What is the most common cause of acute cholangitis?
quiz.a: Gallstones obstructing the common bile duct (Choledocholithiasis).

title: 💪 NURSEMAID'S ELBOW
content:
Radial head subluxation due to sudden pulling on an extended arm.

• Presentation: Child holds arm slightly flexed and pronated; refuses to move it.
• Reduction: Supination and Flexion (Classic) or Hyperpronation.

SVG Visual: Reduction

⚠️ Exam Trap: If the history is classic (pulling injury), X-rays are NOT needed. Proceed directly to reduction. If the child uses the arm normally after 15 min, they can go home.

quiz.q: What anatomical structure is displaced in Nursemaid's elbow?

title: 🩸 VON WILLEBRAND DISEASE (vWD)
content:
The most common inherited bleeding disorder in Canada.

• Symptoms: Mucocutaneous bleeding (Epistaxis, Menorrhagia, easy bruising).
• Labs: Normal PT, Normal or High PTT, Increased Bleeding Time.

⚠️ Exam Trap: Initial treatment for minor bleeding or pre-surgery in Type 1 vWD is Desmopressin (DDAVP), which releases stored vWF from endothelial cells.

quiz.q: What is the pattern of inheritance for the most common type of vWD?

title: 🌊 RUPTURE OF MEMBRANES (PPROM)
content:
Rupture before 37 weeks and before labor begins.

Diagnosis:
1. Nitrazine Test: Blue (Alkaline pH of fluid).
2. Ferning: Microscopic fern-like pattern.


⚠️ Exam Trap: If PPROM occurs < 34 weeks, give Corticosteroids and Antibiotics (to delay labor and prevent infection). Delivery is indicated if there are signs of Chorioamnionitis (Fever + Foul smelling fluid).

quiz.q: What is the most significant risk associated with PPROM?

title: ⚖️ ELDER ABUSE REPORTING
content:
Physical, financial, or emotional abuse of older adults.

• Signs: Unexplained injuries, poor hygiene, sudden changes in a Will.
• Approach: Interview the patient ALONE.

⚠️ Exam Trap: In most Canadian provinces (like Ontario), reporting elder abuse in the community is NOT mandatory if the adult is competent (Autonomy). EXCEPTION: If the elder lives in a Long-term Care home, reporting IS mandatory.

quiz.q: What is the first step when you suspect elder abuse?
quiz.a: Assess the patient's immediate safety and decision-making capacity.

title: 💥 PERFORATED PEPTIC ULCER
content:
A sudden, catastrophic abdominal event.

• Signs: Sudden severe pain, Board-like rigidity, rebound tenderness.
• Dx: Upright Chest X-ray showing Pneumoperitoneum (Free air under diaphragm).

⚠️ Exam Trap: If you see free air under the diaphragm, the next step is NOT more imaging. It is Emergent Surgical Consultation for laparotomy and repair (Graham Patch).

quiz.q: What is the most common site of perforated peptic ulcers?
quiz.a: The anterior wall of the duodenum.

title: 🧩 AUTISM SPECTRUM DISORDER (ASD)
content:
Early identification is key for intervention.

• Core Deficits: Social communication and Restricted/Repetitive behaviors.
• Screening: M-CHAT-R at 18 and 24 months.

⚠️ Exam Trap: A key early sign of ASD in a toddler is a lack of Joint Attention (not pointing at objects to share interest) and failure to respond to their name.

quiz.q: At what age is the Rourke Baby Record recommendation for routine M-CHAT screening?

title: 💊 ASPIRIN (SALICYLATE) POISONING
content:
A complex acid-base disturbance high-yield for exams.

• Classic Pattern: Mixed Respiratory Alkalosis + Anion Gap Metabolic Acidosis.
• Symptoms: Tinnitus (Ringing in ears), Nausea, Hyperventilation.

⚠️ Exam Trap: The goal of treatment is Alkalinization of the Urine (using Sodium Bicarbonate) to trap the salicylate in the urine and speed up excretion. If levels are very high, Hemodialysis is the answer!

quiz.q: What is the earliest symptom of salicylate toxicity?

title: 🥗 ANOREXIA NERVOSA VS BULIMIA
content:
Distinguished primarily by BMI.

FeatureAnorexiaBulimia
BMILow (< 18.5)Normal or Overweight
Key FearGaining weightLoss of control
PhysicalLanugo, AmenorrheaParotid swelling, Russell's sign

⚠️ Exam Trap: If a patient has signs of both but the BMI is low, the diagnosis is ANOREXIA (Binge-purge type). Hospitalization is needed if HR < 40 or BP < 80/50.

quiz.q: What is the most common cause of death in patients with Anorexia Nervosa?
quiz.a: Cardiac complications (Arrhythmias due to electrolyte imbalance) or Suicide.

title: ⚡ REFEEDING SYNDROME
content:
Occurs when food is reintroduced too quickly after starvation.

• Pathophysiology: Surge in Insulin drives electrolytes into cells.
• Hallmark: Hypophosphatemia (Low Phosphate).
• Complication: Heart failure, edema, seizures.

⚠️ Exam Trap: Before refeeding a severely malnourished patient, always check electrolytes and start supplementation, especially Thiamine, to prevent Wernicke's Encephalopathy.

quiz.q: Which electrolyte abnormality is the hallmark of refeeding syndrome?

title: 🦵 HSP (HENOCH-SCHÖNLEIN PURPURA)
content:
IgA-mediated small vessel vasculitis following a URI.

The Classic Tetrad:
1. Palpable Purpura (Buttocks/Legs).
2. Arthralgia (Joint pain).
3. Abdominal Pain (Risk of Intussusception).
4. Renal Disease (Hematuria).


⚠️ Exam Trap: HSP has a NORMAL Platelet Count. If platelets are low, think ITP or Leukemia. Management is supportive (NSAIDs), but use Steroids if GI or Renal involvement is severe.

quiz.q: What is the most common GI complication of HSP?

title: 🫁 LUNG CANCER SCREENING (CANADA)
content:
Canadian Task Force on Preventive Health Care recommendations.

• Who: Adults aged 55 - 74.
• Risk: Current or former smokers with 30 pack-year history.
• Test: Low-dose CT (LDCT) scan.
• Frequency: Every year for 3 consecutive years.

⚠️ Exam Trap: Chest X-ray is NOT used for lung cancer screening (it doesn't reduce mortality). LDCT is the only proven method.

quiz.q: What is the criteria for a former smoker to qualify for screening?
quiz.a: Must have quit within the last 15 years and have a 30 pack-year history.

title: 🍼 BREASTFEEDING CONTRAINDICATIONS
content:
When is it NOT safe to breastfeed in Canada?

• Maternal: HIV infection, Active/untreated TB, Substance abuse.
• Infant: Galactosemia (absolute contraindication).

⚠️ Exam Trap: In Canada, HIV is an absolute contraindication to breastfeeding because safe formula alternatives are available. Hepatitis B or C are NOT contraindications.

quiz.q: Is breastfeeding allowed if the mother has an active Herpes simplex lesion on the breast?
quiz.a: No, breastfeeding should be stopped from that breast until the lesion heals.

title: 🆘 CORD PROLAPSE
content:
Obstetric emergency where the cord descends before the head.

• Sign: Sudden Fetal Bradycardia or deep variable decelerations after ROM.
• Management: Push the head UP (do not touch the cord!) + Knee-chest position.

⚠️ Exam Trap: The first step is to manually elevate the presenting part (fetal head) to relieve pressure on the cord while preparing for an Immediate C-Section.

quiz.q: What is the most common risk factor for umbilical cord prolapse?

title: ⚖️ TRUTH TELLING (CANCER DIAGNOSIS)
content:
When a family asks you NOT to tell the patient they have cancer.

• Canadian Ethic: Respect for Autonomy.
• Rule: You must first ask the patient if they want to know their diagnosis.

⚠️ Exam Trap: You cannot withhold a diagnosis just because the family asks. You only withhold it if the patient explicitly says, "I don't want to know my test results; tell my son instead." (Right NOT to know).

quiz.q: What is the principle that requires physicians to tell the truth to patients?

title: 💥 AAA RUPTURE (TRIAD)
content:
Abdominal Aortic Aneurysm rupture is a surgical catastrophe.

The Classic Triad:
1. Sudden severe Abdominal/Back Pain.
2. Hypotension (Shock).
3. Pulsatile Abdominal Mass.

⚠️ Exam Trap: If the patient is Unstable with the triad, do NOT wait for a CT scan. The answer is Immediate Laparotomy/OR. CT is only for stable patients.

quiz.q: What size of AAA in a male patient generally requires elective surgical repair?

title: 🧠 SSRI DISCONTINUATION SYNDROME
content:
Occurs when antidepressants are stopped abruptly (Common with Paroxetine).

• Mnemonic: FINISH
• F: Flu-like symptoms.
• I: Insomnia.
• N: Nausea.
• I: Imbalance.
• S: Sensory (Zaps/Tingling).
• H: Hyperarousal.

⚠️ Exam Trap: This is not 'addiction' or 'relapse'. It's a physiological response. To avoid this, Taper the medication slowly over several weeks.

quiz.q: Which SSRI has the lowest risk of discontinuation syndrome due to its long half-life?

title: 🤱 POSTPARTUM MOOD DISORDERS
content:
A high-yield distinction for both OB and Psych sections.

FeatureBaby BluesPostpartum DepressionPostpartum Psychosis
Onset2-3 days4-6 weeksDays to 2 weeks
DurationEnds by 2 weeksMonthsEmergency
SeverityMild / FunctionalModerate / ImpairedSevere / Danger

⚠️ Exam Trap: Postpartum Psychosis is a psychiatric emergency. The risk of infanticide is high. The correct answer is IMMEDIATE Hospitalization and separation (under supervision) until stabilized.

quiz.q: What is the primary treatment for Postpartum Blues?

title: 🌀 MIDGUT VOLVULUS (MALROTATION)
content:
Surgical emergency in neonates.

• Presentation: Bilious Vomiting in the first week of life.
• Imaging: Upper GI series (Gold Standard) showing 'Corkscrew' appearance.
• U/S: Whirlpool sign.


⚠️ Exam Trap: Bilious vomiting in a neonate is Volvulus until proven otherwise. Do not waste time with basic blood tests; get an Upper GI Series or consult surgery immediately!

quiz.q: What is the definitive surgical procedure for midgut volvulus?

title: ⚖️ GIFTS FROM PATIENTS
content:
Canadian ethical guidelines on professional boundaries.

• Small Gifts: (e.g., box of chocolates) Generally acceptable if no strings attached.
• Large/Expensive Gifts: (e.g., money, jewelry) Must be refused.

⚠️ Exam Trap: If a patient offers a large sum of money, the answer is NOT just to refuse. You must Explain the professional boundary and document the conversation. If they insist, suggest a donation to the hospital foundation instead.

quiz.q: What is the primary concern with accepting expensive gifts from patients?
quiz.a: It can compromise professional objectivity and create a 'debt' dynamic.

title: 💓 FETAL HEART RATE (VEAL CHOP)
content:
Decoding the electronic fetal monitor (EFM).

• Variable → Cord Compression.
• Early → Head Compression.
• Accelerations → Okay (Oxygenated).
• Late → Placental Insufficiency.

⚠️ Exam Trap: Late Decelerations are the most concerning. If they are repetitive, the first step is Maternal Positioning (Left Lateral), Oxygen, and IV fluids. If they persist, prepare for Delivery.

quiz.q: What is the classic cause of early decelerations?

title: 🚬 CARBON MONOXIDE (CO) POISONING
content:
The 'Silent Killer' in Canadian winters (faulty heaters).

• Symptoms: Headache, dizziness, 'Cherry-red' skin (rare).
• Diagnosis: Carboxyhemoglobin level (ABG/VBG). Pulse ox is useless (reads 100%).

⚠️ Exam Trap: Treat with 100% Oxygen until asymptomatic. Indications for Hyperbaric Oxygen include: Pregnancy, CO-Hgb > 25%, or Neurological symptoms (loss of consciousness).

quiz.q: Why is Pulse Oximetry misleading in CO poisoning?
quiz.a: The device cannot distinguish between Oxyhemoglobin and Carboxyhemoglobin.

title: 🔪 DIVERTICULITIS: CLINICAL STEPS
content:
Management based on severity.

• Uncomplicated: Oral Abx + Liquid diet (Outpatient).
• Complicated (Abscess/Perforation): IV Abx + Admission.

⚠️ Exam Trap: If CT shows an abscess > 3 cm, the next step is Percutaneous Drainage (IR-guided). If there is free air (Perforation), the answer is Emergency Surgery (Hartmann’s Procedure).

quiz.q: Which part of the colon is most commonly affected by diverticulitis?

title: 👶 FOREIGN BODY ASPIRATION
content:
Sudden onset of choking and wheezing in a toddler.

• Signs: Unilateral wheezing, decreased breath sounds.
• X-ray: Expiratory film shows air trapping (Lung remains hyperinflated).

⚠️ Exam Trap: If a child has persistent pneumonia in the SAME lobe, think Foreign Body. The definitive diagnosis and treatment is Rigid Bronchoscopy.

quiz.q: Where is the most common location for an aspirated foreign body?

title: 🦠 BACTERIAL VAGINOSIS (AMSEL)
content:
Differentiating vaginal discharge.

Amsel Criteria (Need 3/4):
1. Thin, homogeneous white discharge.
2. Clue Cells on microscopy.
3. pH > 4.5.
4. Positive Whiff Test (Fishy odor with KOH).

⚠️ Exam Trap: BV is NOT an STI. You do NOT need to treat the male partner. Treatment is Metronidazole (Oral or Gel).

quiz.q: What is the hallmark cell seen on wet mount for BV?
quiz.a: Clue Cells (epithelial cells covered in bacteria).

title: 🍄 WERNICKE ENCEPHALOPATHY
content:
Acute Thiamine (B1) deficiency in alcoholics.

The Classic Triad:
1. Ataxia (Unsteady gait).
2. Ophthalmoplegia (Nystagmus/Abnormal eye moves).
3. Confusion.

⚠️ Exam Trap: ALWAYS give Thiamine BEFORE Glucose. If you give Glucose first, it will consume the remaining B1 and trigger permanent brain damage (Korsakoff Syndrome).

quiz.q: Which part of the brain is characteristically affected in Wernicke's?

title: ⚖️ APOLOGIZING FOR ERRORS
content:
Canadian (CMPA) approach to medical errors.

• Full Disclosure: Tell the patient what happened, the consequences, and the plan to prevent it.
• The Apology: It is encouraged.

⚠️ Exam Trap: In most Canadian provinces (Apology Acts), a sincere apology is NOT admissible in court as an admission of liability. Don't be afraid to say "I am sorry this happened to you."

quiz.q: When should disclosure of an adverse event occur?
quiz.a: As soon as possible after the event is recognized.

title: 🦴 COMPARTMENT SYNDROME
content:
A limb-threatening surgical emergency! Pressure inside fascia exceeds perfusion pressure.

The 6 P's (Late signs):
Pain, Paresthesia, Pallor, Paralysis, Pulselessness, Poikilothermia.

⚠️ Exam Trap: The EARLIEST and most reliable sign is Pain out of proportion to the injury and Pain with passive stretch of the muscles. Do not wait for pulselessness (that's too late!). Treatment: Immediate Fasciotomy.

quiz.q: What is the earliest clinical indicator of compartment syndrome?
quiz.a: Pain out of proportion to the injury and pain on passive stretching.

title: 🤒 FEBRILE SEIZURES (SIMPLE VS COMPLEX)
content:
Common in children 6 months to 5 years.

FeatureSimpleComplex
Duration< 15 minutes> 15 minutes
TypeGeneralizedFocal
RecurrenceOnce in 24hMultiple in 24h

⚠️ Exam Trap: Management for simple febrile seizure is Reassurance and treating the fever source. No long-term anti-epileptics or imaging required. Risk of developing epilepsy is only ~1-2% (same as general pop).

quiz.q: What is the age range for febrile seizures?

title: 🌡️ POSTPARTUM ENDOMETRITIS
content:
The #1 cause of fever after delivery (especially after C-section).

• Signs: Fever (>38°C), Uterine tenderness, Foul-smelling lochia.
• Pathogens: Polymicrobial (Gram+, Gram-, Anaerobes).

⚠️ Exam Trap: The gold standard treatment is IV Clindamycin + Gentamicin. Treatment continues until the patient is afebrile for 24-48 hours.

quiz.q: What is the single most important risk factor for postpartum endometritis?

title: ⚖️ MANDATORY REPORTING (VARIOUS)
content:
When 'Public Interest' overrides 'Patient Confidentiality' in Canada.

Must Report:
1. Infectious Diseases: To Public Health (Syphilis, HIV, TB).
2. Aeronautical/Pilot: If unfit to fly (Transport Canada).
3. Gunshot Wounds: To Police.
4. Child/Elder Abuse: (As covered before).

⚠️ Exam Trap: If a patient tells you they were sexually assaulted, you do NOT report to police without their consent (unless they are a minor or at imminent risk to others). Autonomy is key here.

quiz.q: Do you need patient consent to report a gunshot wound in Ontario?
quiz.a: No, it is a mandatory legal requirement for hospitals.

title: 🌬️ COPD STABLE MANAGEMENT (GOLD)
content:
Canadian Thoracic Society guidelines focus on symptom control.

• Mild (Few symptoms): SABA or SAMA (Ventolin/Atrovent).
• Moderate (Persistent): Add LAMA (Tiotropium) or LABA.
• Severe (Exacerbations): LAMA + LABA + ICS.

⚠️ Exam Trap: The only interventions proven to reduce mortality in COPD are: Smoking Cessation and Long-term Oxygen Therapy (if PaO2 < 55 mmHg). Inhalers improve symptoms/exacerbations but not survival!

quiz.q: What is the most effective way to slow the decline of FEV1 in COPD?

title: 🔥 POST-OPERATIVE FEVER (THE 5 W's)
content:
Timing of fever after surgery helps narrow the cause.

1. Wind (Day 1-2): Atelectasis / Pneumonia.
2. Water (Day 3): UTI.
3. Walking (Day 5): DVT / PE.
4. Wound (Day 7): Surgical site infection.
5. Wonder Drugs: Drug-induced fever.

⚠️ Exam Trap: If a patient develops high fever (>40°C) and muscle rigidity immediately after induction of anesthesia, it's Malignant Hyperthermia (Tx: Dantrolene).

quiz.q: What is the most likely cause of fever on post-op day 1?

title: 🧪 CKD STAGING & REFERRAL
content:
Chronic Kidney Disease based on GFR.

• Stage 1: GFR > 90 (Damage with normal GFR).
• Stage 3: GFR 30-59 (Moderate).
• Stage 5: GFR < 15 (Kidney Failure / Dialysis).

⚠️ Exam Trap: When to refer to Nephrology? In Canada, refer if GFR < 30, rapid decline (>5 mL/min/yr), or resistant hypertension.

quiz.q: What is the first-line antihypertensive for a CKD patient with proteinuria?

title: 🌞 NEONATAL JAUNDICE (PATHOLOGIC)
content:
Differentiating Benign from Danger.

• Physiologic: Appears AFTER 24 hours, resolves by 1-2 weeks.
• Pathologic: Appears WITHIN 24 hours, Conjugated Bilirubin is HIGH, or persists > 2 weeks.

⚠️ Exam Trap: If jaundice occurs in the first 24 hours of life, the answer is Hemolysis (ABO/Rh incompatibility) or infection. This is always an emergency!

quiz.q: What is the most feared complication of severe unconjugated hyperbilirubinemia?

title: 🚑 ANAPHYLAXIS MANAGEMENT
content:
Rapid, life-threatening allergic reaction.

Priority Steps:
1. IM Epinephrine (1:1000): Mid-outer thigh (0.3mg adult, 0.15mg child).
2. Airway/Oxygen.
3. IV Fluid Bolus (if hypotensive).
4. Adjuncts: Salbutamol (for wheeze), Antihistamines, Steroids.

⚠️ Exam Trap: Do NOT wait for 'Stridor' or 'Shock' to give Epinephrine. If there is involvement of two organ systems (e.g., Hives + Vomiting) after exposure, give it NOW!

quiz.q: What is the dose and concentration of IM Epinephrine for an adult in anaphylaxis?

title: 🧠 SCHIZOPHRENIA VS SCHIZOAFFECTIVE
content:
FeatureSchizophreniaSchizoaffective
Core SymptomsPsychosis (Delusions/Hallucinations)Psychosis PLUS Mood (Mania/Depression)
Duration> 6 monthsVaries
Key RuleNo prominent mood episodesPsychosis for 2 weeks WITHOUT mood

⚠️ Exam Trap: To diagnose Schizoaffective, the patient must have had hallucinations/delusions for at least 2 weeks in the absence of a major mood episode (Depression or Mania).

quiz.q: How long must symptoms last to meet the DSM-5 criteria for Schizophrenia?
quiz.a: At least 6 months (including 1 month of active symptoms).

quiz.a: Pain out of proportion to the injury and pain on passive stretching.

title: 🧠 SCHIZOPHRENIA VS SCHIZOAFFECTIVE
content:
FeatureSchizophreniaSchizoaffective
Core SymptomsPsychosis (Delusions/Hallucinations)Psychosis PLUS Mood (Mania/Depression)
Duration> 6 monthsVaries
Key RuleNo prominent mood episodesPsychosis for 2 weeks WITHOUT mood

⚠️ Exam Trap: To diagnose Schizoaffective, the patient must have had hallucinations/delusions for at least 2 weeks in the absence of a major mood episode (Depression or Mania).

quiz.q: How long must symptoms last to meet the DSM-5 criteria for Schizophrenia?
quiz.a: At least 6 months (including 1 month of active symptoms).
